9: The seven hills on which the woman sits (Revelation 17:9: hepta ore) is the most explicit historical identification in Revelation — Rome was universally known as the 'city of seven hills' (Septemontium). The woman 'Babylon the great' (Revelation 17:5, 18: 'the great city that has dominion over the kings of the earth') is Rome — coded in the 'Babylon' cipher that 1 Peter also uses (1 Peter 5:13). Jewish and Christian apocalyptic literature routinely used 'Babylon' for Rome as the current oppressive empire (4 Ezra 3:1-2, 28-31; 2 Baruch 10-11). The coding protected the text from Roman censorship while being transparent to readers in the seven churches who knew the 'mystery' (Revelation 17:7).
1: 'The great prostitute who sits by many waters' introduces an allegory drawing on the OT prophetic tradition of characterizing pagan cities as promiscuous women who seduce the nations into spiritual unfaithfulness: Nineveh (Nahum 3:4), Tyre (Isaiah 23:17), and above all Babylon (Isaiah 21:9; Jer 50-51). By the late first century CE, 'Babylon' had become an established cipher for Rome in Jewish and early Christian literature. The Sibylline Oracles, 4 Ezra (Revelation 3:1-2, 28-31), 2 Baruch (Revelation 10:1-3; Revelation 11:1), and 1 Peter 5:13 all use Babylon as a code for Rome — an audience in the seven cities of Asia would recognize the identification immediately without John stating it explicitly.
2: 'All the nations have drunk the maddening wine of her adulteries' describes Rome's hegemonic cultural and economic influence across the Mediterranean world. In the OT prophetic tradition, 'adultery' and 'prostitution' describe spiritual unfaithfulness to YHWH — covenant betrayal through accommodation to foreign gods (Jeremiah 3:1-10; Ezek 16; Hos 1-3). Applied to Rome's client nations, the image captures the way Roman imperial ideology, cult, and commerce penetrated every dimension of subject-peoples' lives. The 'wine' of intoxication: those who profited from the imperial system became drunk on its benefits, unable to see its corrupting nature.
3: The 'scarlet beast covered with blasphemous names' on which the woman rides is the same beast as in Revelation 13 — the Roman imperial power. The seven heads and ten horns identify it with the beast from the sea (Revelation 13:1). The wilderness setting for the vision ('carried me away in the Spirit into a wilderness') echoes the wilderness as the place of prophetic encounter (Ezekiel 3:22; cf. the desert as the place of theophany). In the Exodus tradition, the wilderness was also the place where Israel encountered both God and the temptation to return to Egypt — the wilderness vision of Rome's doom mirrors the wilderness encounters of the prophets who spoke against Babylon and Egypt.
4: Purple and scarlet were the colors of Roman imperial and senatorial dress. The senatorial toga had a broad purple stripe; military commanders wore the paludamentum (a scarlet military cloak); and the emperor himself wore purple robes. In Revelation's audience cities, purple cloth was a luxury trade good (Lydia of Thyatira sold it in Philippi, Acts 16:14). The golden cup filled with 'abominations and the filth of her adulteries' inverts the golden vessels of the Jerusalem temple — Belshazzar's use of temple vessels to toast his gods (Daniel 5:1-4) is the type of this inversion: desacralizing the holy by using sacred vessels for profane purposes.
5: The 'mystery name' on the woman's forehead echoes the practice of Roman prostitutes displaying their names on their foreheads (Seneca, Controversiae 1.2.7), while simultaneously employing the apocalyptic convention of revealed names as ciphers (Daniel 4:19-27). 'Babylon the Great' was recognizable as Rome: the Sibylline Oracles use it repeatedly (Sib. Or. Revelation 5:143, 158-161, 434); 4 Ezra 3:1-2 opens with the author claiming to be in 'Babylon' (= Rome). 'Mother of Prostitutes and of the Abominations of the Earth' echoes Nahum's characterization of Nineveh as a prostitute whose witchcraft enslaved nations (Nahum 3:4) — Rome as the newest iteration of this historical pattern.
6: 'Drunk with the blood of God's holy people and the blood of those who bore testimony to Jesus' refers to the Roman persecutions. The Neronian persecution of 64 CE is the most documented: after the fire of Rome, Nero blamed Christians and subjected them to elaborate public executions (Tacitus, Annales 15.44 — burned alive as human torches, thrown to wild animals, crucified). The apostles Peter and Paul were executed in Rome during this period. The Domitianic persecution (81-96 CE) — the probable period of Revelation's composition — targeted members of the Roman household who converted (Flavia Domitilla, exiled; Flavius Clemens, executed). John's astonishment ('I was greatly astonished') captures the psychological shock of seeing Rome's immense power revealed as a drunk murderess.
7: The angel's offer to explain the mystery is the standard apocalyptic interpretation scene: Daniel receives interpretations from Gabriel (Daniel 7:15-16; Revelation 8:15-19); the angels of 1 Enoch explain heavenly visions to Enoch. The pattern establishes the apocalyptic seer as a mediator who receives not just visions but their authoritative interpretation — the reader receives the same explanation the seer did, making the community participants in the disclosure.
8: The 'beast that once was, now is not, and yet will come up out of the Abyss' parodies the divine self-description ('who was and is and is to come,' Revelation 1:4, Revelation 4:8) while encoding the Nero redivivus legend. After Nero's suicide in 68 CE, a widespread belief emerged (documented in the Sibylline Oracles: Revelation 4:119-124; Revelation 5:28-34, 137-154, 214-227, 361-383) that he had fled to Parthia and would return at the head of a Parthian army. Three imposters claiming to be Nero appeared in 69, 80, and c. 88 CE. This expectation gave urgency to the 'once was, now is not, will come' formula — a historical cipher for the anticipated return of the persecutor. The community 'whose name was not written in the book of life' is embedded in the imperial order, astonished at Rome's apparent permanence.
10: 'Five have fallen, one is, and the other has not yet come' refers to the sequence of Roman emperors. The precise identification depends on which emperor one begins counting from. Beginning with Augustus (27 BCE – 14 CE): Augustus (1), Tiberius (2), Caligula (3), Claudius (4), Nero (5) — five fallen; Vespasian (6) — one is; Titus (7) — not yet come; Domitian (8) — the eighth (Revelation 17:11). This count fits a composition date of 69-79 CE. Starting with Caligula or Julius Caesar produces different results. The text's deliberate ambiguity may be intentional — the formula applies to any moment in the empire's history when five are past and the end is near.
11: 'The beast who once was and now is not is an eighth king. He belongs to the seven and is going to his destruction.' The eighth who 'belongs to the seven' reflects the Nero redivivus expectation: Nero was the fifth emperor in one count; when he returns from Parthia (in the legend) he becomes a new figure who is simultaneously one of the original seven. The 'going to his destruction' (eis apōleian) is the same destination as the false prophet and the beast (Revelation 19:20; Revelation 20:10) — eternal judgment, not renewed power. The Nero redivivus myth that frightened the seven cities is met with the assurance that the returning tyrant is already heading to destruction.
12: The 'ten kings who have not yet received a kingdom' are the Parthian satellite kings or military commanders who, in the Nero redivivus scenario, would provide the returning Nero with his army. The Roman fear of Parthia was real: Rome had suffered a catastrophic defeat at Carrhae (53 BCE) in which the general Crassus was killed and the legionary standards captured; Augustus had to negotiate their return diplomatically. The Euphrates as the boundary between Rome and Parthia (Revelation 16:12) was the empire's most vulnerable frontier. The 'one hour' of their authority captures the brevity and provisional nature of their power — granted only for the purpose of God's judgment.
13: The unity of purpose among the beast's allies ('they have one purpose') contrasts with Rome's historical pattern of factional conflict — the Year of Four Emperors (69 CE), the civil wars, the Praetorian Guard's repeated selling of the imperial throne to the highest bidder. The ironically unified purpose of the beast's coalition is to wage war against the Lamb — and this very unity is the mechanism of their defeat, since God has put it into their hearts to accomplish his purpose (Revelation 17:17).
14: 'They will wage war against the Lamb, but the Lamb will triumph' — the victory formula uses 'Lord of lords and King of kings,' titles with a specific ancient Near Eastern background. The Persian king was called 'king of kings' (melek malkayya, Ezra 7:12; Ezekiel 26:7; Daniel 2:37) — a claim to universal sovereignty over all other rulers. Antiochus IV Epiphanes used the title; Roman emperors claimed equivalent lordship. The Lamb's identification as 'Lord of lords and King of kings' is the ultimate counter-claim: all the king-of-kings claims of history find their true referent only in him.
15: 'The waters you saw, where the prostitute sits, are peoples, multitudes, nations, and languages.' Rome's power rested on its ability to integrate diverse ethnic and linguistic populations into the imperial order. The city of Rome itself was home to significant populations from across the Mediterranean world: Greek-speakers from the East formed a substantial community (early Roman Christianity was largely Greek-speaking), and Jews, Syrians, Egyptians, and North Africans all had significant communities in the capital. The 'many waters' is thus a geopolitically accurate description of Rome's cosmopolitan subject peoples.
16: The beast and ten horns turning on the prostitute to 'bring her to ruin and leave her naked; they will eat her flesh and burn her with fire' draws on the OT judgment formula applied to idolatrous women (Ezekiel 16:37-41; Revelation 23:25-29). Rome's actual history of civil war, assassination, and factional violence lent credibility to this scenario: the Year of Four Emperors (69 CE), the assassination of Caligula (41 CE), Domitian himself (96 CE), and the Praetorian Guard's repeated violent regime changes all demonstrated that the imperial power was internally self-destructive. Empires that live by violence tend to destroy themselves by violence.
17: 'God has put it into their hearts to accomplish his purpose by agreeing to hand over their royal authority to the beast until God's words are fulfilled.' A recurring theological principle in Revelation: even the hostile powers act within the permission and purpose of God. The parallel is to Isaiah 10:5-7 (Assyria as God's rod of judgment against Israel, though Assyria does not intend this) and Jeremiah 25:9 (Nebuchadnezzar called God's servant for the purpose of judgment). The apocalyptic framework does not portray history as an even contest between God and evil but as God's sovereign use of even evil's self-destructive tendencies for his own purposes.
18: 'The woman you saw is the great city that rules over the kings of the earth.' The explicit identification of the woman with the city removes any lingering ambiguity: this is Rome, the city that in John's era unambiguously 'ruled the kings of the earth.' The seven hills (Revelation 17:9), the scarlet imperial dress (Revelation 17:4), and the blood of martyrs (Revelation 17:6) all point to the same identification. The cipher of Babylon allowed Jews and Christians to speak of Rome's political reality in language legible to insiders but opaque to hostile authorities — a standard feature of apocalyptic literature from Daniel (written under Antiochus IV) through the Sibylline Oracles (written under various empires).
